answer:
**Charles Comte's *Traité de Législation: VOL I* (Chapter 19)** argues precisely this.[^1]

There, Comte writes: "perhaps one would arrive at this consequence, **that a people already civilized needs, to be happy, only not to be pillaged, and to be abandoned to itself**." He immediately continues by explaining that such a people "would do better by the sole force of its customs... than our learned politicians can do."[^2]

A few paragraphs earlier, he observes that "governments, **so weak when they wish to do good, sometimes possess an immense influence for doing evil**," after analyzing how governmental interventions often produce more harm than natural customs or instincts alone.[^3]

[^1]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL I*, [Chapter 19](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/19-des-effets-particuliers-a-chacun-des-principaux-el/translation/).

[^2]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL I*, [paragraph](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/19-des-effets-particuliers-a-chacun-des-principaux-el/translation/#p-45347c).

[^3]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL I*, [paragraph](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/19-des-effets-particuliers-a-chacun-des-principaux-el/translation/#p-dc2a16).

args.query: not to be pillaged, and to be abandoned to itself
"governments are far more powerful at doing harm than at doing good"
